questions.prompt:
(25 points) Martin just finished a long novel with n chapters, where chapter i is ni pages
long. The publisher told Martin that publishing each book costs C dollars, and they allow a
maximum number of P pages in each book. The chapters must appear in sequential order.
(a) (15 points) Suppose Martin wishes to minimize the total cost of publishing his novel,
i.e., minimizing the number of books needed to publish his novel. He decides to greedily
fit the first k1 chapters into the first book for the largest possible k1, i.e., I:;t,1 ni :S
P < I:7;,t1 ni, and then fit as many chapters as possible into the second, and so forth.
Does his greedy approach always minimize the number of books? Why or why not?
(b) ( 10 points) Martin does not want to break the story at unnatural points. Suppose
Martin can quantify the cost c(i) (in terms of dollars) of breaking chapters i and i + 1
into two books. In other words, if the first book ends at chapter k1, the second book
ends at chapter k2, and so forth, until the last book m ends at chapter km = n, the
total cost would be C-m+c(k1)+c(k2)+· · ·+c(km_i). Design a dynamic programming
algorithm to help Martin minimize this cost, and argue for the correctness and running
time of your algorithm.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) HKU will launch a new undergraduate program in "Future Technologies." The
program director, Alice, has come up with a preliminary design of the curriculum. We use
a directed graph G = (V, E) to represent the courses and prerequisite requirements each
course i corresponds to a vertex i E V, and let there be a directed edge from i to j if course
i is a prerequisite to course j.
Before submitting the curriculum for the university's approval, Alice would like to run a
few tests. She has already used DFS to verify that graph G is acyclic, i.e., the curriculum
is free of cyclic prerequisite requirements. Next, she wants to know if there is any directed
path with strictly more than k = 8 vertices/courses on it. If such a path exists, then the
students would not be able to complete their studies within 8 semesters.
Can you help Alice design a polynomial-time (in IVI, IEI, and a general value of k) algorithm
for her problem? To get full credit, you need to argue for the correctness and running time
of the algorithm.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) This question considers the Weighted Interval Scheduling problem. There are
n jobs. Each job 1 ::::; j ::=:; n has a start time Sj 2 0, finish time Jj > Sj, and a positive
weighte Wj > 0. Two jobs j and j' are compatible if their intervals (sj, Jj) and (sj,, fF) are
disjoint. We hope to find a set of mutually compatible jobs such that the total weight of
these jobs is maximized. Design a polynomial-time algorithm for this problem, and argue
for the correctness and running time of your algorithm.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) The School of Computing and Data Science needs to decide on the teaching
assignment for the 2025-26 academic year. A set of undergraduate (UG) courses U and
postgraduate (PG) courses P will be offered. There are n teachers. Each teacher 1 ::; i ::; n
has his/her own expertise and can teach only a subset of the courses. Let Ui ~ U and
Pi ~ P be the subsets of UG and PG courses teacher i can teach. Each teacher shall teach
3 courses, within which there must be at least 1 UG course. Each course needs to be taught
by a teacher. (You may assume IUI + jPj = 3n.) Design a polynomial-time algorithm for
finding a feasible teaching assignment whenever one exists.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) Suppose there are n portfolios, each of which is associated with a pair of non­
negative numbers (ai, bi), where ai 2 0 is the return in one year, and bi 2 0 is the return in
ten years. You can also invest in multiple portfolios by splitting your assets. For example,
investing one-third and two-thirds in the first two portfolios yields ai ~ 2a 2 in one year, and
bi ~Zbz in ten years. Given a pair of targets (A, B), you want to know whether you can get
a return of at least A in one year, and a return of at least B in ten years.
(a) (10 points) Show that it suffices to consider pairs of portfolios. That is, if the targets
are achievable, then there are two portfolios i and j and a fraction p E [O, 1], such that:
p · ai + (1 - p) · aj 2 A, p ·bi+ (1 - p) · bj 2 B .
Use this fact to design an O(n 2 ) time algorithm.
(b) (15 points) Design an O(nlogn) divide and conquer algorithm for this problem.
